coredata.dc:title: Utilization of biodiesel residue through efficient microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots: A versatile nanomaterial for environmental remediation

coredata.dc:description: The rapid expansion of the biodiesel industry has substantially increased crude glycerol residue (CG) production, creating sustainability and economic challenges due to surplus glycerol generation. Conventional purification methods are costly and environmentally demanding, necessitating innovative strategies to utilize this residue effectively. This study innovates by exploring the microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from CG, exemplifying a shift toward sustainable biodiesel production by transforming the residue into a multifunctional material. CDs exhibited a nanometric spherical morphology of 2.6 ± 0.3 nm diameter verified by HRTEM analysis. The reduction of oxygen confirmed the transformation of CG into CDs–H bands and the formation of polyaromatic structures. The graphitic structures were verified by Raman spectroscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance, and X-ray diffraction. The quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 1.3%. The multifunctionality of CDs has been proven in sensing and photocatalysis. In the sensor applications, the CDs demonstrated strong fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, with a limit of detection of 1.80 ± 0.01 and 4.00 ± 0.04 mg.L−1, respectively, indicating the potential use of CDs in detecting these ions in wastewater samples. When complexed with titanium dioxide (TiO2), the TiO2/CDs composite showed an extended photoresponse of TiO2 to the UV–visible region, improving the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions for Victoria Blue B (VBB) dye degradation (98% degradation after 150 min). This research highlights the potential of utilizing CG to produce versatile CDs, contribute to more sustainable biodiesel production, and offer promising applications in environmental sensing and photocatalysis.

head.ce:title: Utilization of biodiesel residue through efficient microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots: A versatile nanomaterial for environmental remediation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid expansion of the biodiesel industry has substantially increased crude glycerol residue (CG) production, creating sustainability and economic challenges due to surplus glycerol generation. Conventional purification methods are costly and environmentally demanding, necessitating innovative strategies to utilize this residue effectively. This study innovates by exploring the microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from CG, exemplifying a shift toward sustainable biodiesel production by transforming the residue into a multifunctional material. CDs exhibited a nanometric spherical morphology of 2.6 ± 0.3 nm diameter verified by HRTEM analysis. The reduction of oxygen confirmed the transformation of CG into CDs–H bands and the formation of polyaromatic structures. The graphitic structures were verified by Raman spectroscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance, and X-ray diffraction. The quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 1.3%. The multifunctionality of CDs has been proven in sensing and photocatalysis. In the sensor applications, the CDs demonstrated strong fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, with a limit of detection of 1.80 ± 0.01 and 4.00 ± 0.04 mg.L−1, respectively, indicating the potential use of CDs in detecting these ions in wastewater samples. When complexed with titanium dioxide (TiO2), the TiO2/CDs composite showed an extended photoresponse of TiO2 to the UV–visible region, improving the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions for Victoria Blue B (VBB) dye degradation (98% degradation after 150 min). This research highlights the potential of utilizing CG to produce versatile CDs, contribute to more sustainable biodiesel production, and offer promising applications in environmental sensing and photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth in biodiesel production has led to a significant increase in crude glycerol residue (CG) generation (1.0 kg of glycerol generated per 10 kg of biodiesel produced), presenting challenges to the sustainability and economic viability of the biodiesel industry (Wang et al., 2024). This surplus of CG affects market prices and necessitates innovative approaches to address this residue and effectively utilize it as a renewable bioresource (Monteiro et al., 2018). Conventional purification methods for CG are costly and environmentally difficult, prompting a shift towards technologies that directly utilize CG. This shift is aimed at making biodiesel production more sustainable and economically feasible. Exploring novel methods like producing sustainable quantum dots from CG exemplifies this new era of bioresource utilization, enhancing the efficiency and sustainability of the biodiesel industry (Wang et al., 2024).

Materials known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs or CDs) are nanometer-scale semiconductors with specific electrical and optical properties. Due to their dimensions smaller than 10 nm, these materials present charge carriers (electron-hole pairs) in a state of solid confinement called quantum confinement, which allows absorption and emission in the visible, ultraviolet, and near-infrared regions (Yu Yao et al., 2022). Its carbon composition is a facilitator in choosing the precursor material, as several materials of natural origin rich in carbon can be used, such as glucose, sucrose, starch, and glycerol (Ullal et al., 2022; Uriarte et al., 2019).

CDs preparation methods can be divided into two categories: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down approach indicates that the process starts from a large structure (macroscopic scale) to nanostructures, including methods such as laser ablation, chemical ablation, electrochemical, and plasma treatment. In bottom-up methods, the process starts with atoms that constitute bonds until the formation of the nanomaterial; techniques such as pyrolysis, microwave irradiation, chemical oxidation, and hydrothermal can be mentioned (Atchudan et al., 2020; Du et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2018b; Yu Yao et al., 2022). In literature there are several raw materials and methods of the CDs preparation (Atchudan et al., 2018, 2019, 2020, 2022).

The microwave irradiation approach offers advantages such as rapid volumetric heating of carbon precursors, which reduces reaction time and energy consumption. It is an easy operation method associated with high yield and efficiency (Feng et al., 2021). Literature relates the use of microwaves to obtain CDs that use pure glycerol as a carbon source (Huang et al., 2014b; Uriarte et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2011). When subjected to microwave-assisted pyrolysis, glycerol molecules undergo dehydration, then pyrolysis, where the products crosslink, forming carbon nanoparticles (Huang et al., 2014b). To date, the CDs can be prepared using glycerol with high purity, with CG being a topic to explore. In other words, to date, no studies have used CG as a microwave for CDs production, making this the innovative focus of this study.

The versatility of CDs also encompasses fluorescence property, which can be used in sensing of various substances by exhibiting fluorescence quenching or extinction (Ashley and Manikova, 2024). The alterations in their fluorescence intensity when exposed to certain chemical compounds offer opportunities for advancing analytical methods for several environmental pollutants, including ions such as Cu2+ and Fe3+ (He et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2017).

Another possible application of CDs is in pollutant photodegradation. This nanomaterial can be used as photocatalyst material or forming CDs-based composite photocatalysts with traditional semiconductors (for example, TiO2, ZnO, Fe2O3, Cu2O, and Bi2O3) (Long et al., 2021). The excitation of CDs - permits the generation and transition of excitons from the valence band to the conduction band, generating electrons/holes. From this, the CDs may drive photocatalytic redox reactions (Zhou et al., 2019). In conjunction with other semiconductors, which have a band gap limited to absorption in the UV region, the use of CDs offers an expansion of the range of absorbed energy, with the possibility of absorption in the UV–visible region, extending the photoresponse of the photocatalyst. Also, the CDs can trap and transfer electrons, decreasing the recombination of photogenerated charges (Long et al., 2021).

The current study aims to develop sustainable and versatile CDs derived from CG using microwave pyrolysis. These CDs were thoroughly investigated and tested as ion sensors using the fluorometric method. Additionally, the study examined the interaction between CDs and titanium dioxide (TiO2) for photocatalysis. Various techniques were used to analyze the physical-chemical properties of the CDs and the CDs/TiO2 composite. The multifunctionality of synthesized CDs has been proven in sensing Fe3+ and Cu2+ and the photocatalysis of Victoria Blue B (VBB) dye.

CDs were prepared using CG as a precursor, according to the methodology of Jiang et al., with modifications in heating time, the inclusion of heating cycles and pauses, and the carbon source, considering that analytical grade glycerol was used in the authors' study (Jiang et al., 2015). First, CG was heated at 70 °C for 1 h to evaporate residual alcohol. A mixture of 3 mL CG and 7 mL of deionized water was used to prepare the CDs. This mixture was heated in a microwave oven (PMM24, Philco), using a power of 240 W. Different cycles and interval times were tested, establishing the following conditions: sequence of heating cycles with pause intervals of 1 min, obtaining two materials with total heating time of 21 min (sequence of cycles: 1.5 + 1.5 + 3 + 6 + 9 min) and 30 min (sequence of cycles: 1.5 + 1.5 + 3 + 6 + 9 + 9 min), which were named CDs_21 and CDs_30, respectively.

CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (Tecnai G2-20 Transmission Electron Microscope - FEI SuperTwin 200 kV). Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique (Vertex 70 spectrometer, Bruker). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) (Bruker Avance III™ HD 600 MHz spectrometer, Bruker). Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR Evolution, Horiba) with a 514 nm excitation laser. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) (EMX Plus X-band EPR spectrometer, Bruker) with MW frequency ∼9.8 GHz. X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Ultima IV diffractometer, Rigaku). UV–visible absorption (V-730 spectrophotometer, Jasco), and UV–visible fluorescence emission measurements (FS-5 spectrofluorometer, Edinburgh Instruments). Quantum yield (QY) of CDs_30 was calculated by using quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate (Sigma-Aldrich) solution (solvent: 0.1 mol L−1 of H2SO4) as a reference and following the equation:

where QY is the sample quantum yield, QYR is the reference compound quantum yield, η is the refractive index of the solvent, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, and A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. The absorbances are optimally kept in A < 0.05 to avoid inner filter effects. Photostability of the CDs was evaluated under UV irradiation (Lamps Philips Blacklight Blue-18 W, λ
max = 360 nm) for 180 min, and fluorescence spectra were recorded.

To evaluate the sensitivity of CDs to metal ions, CDs solutions of 2.10−3 mg L−1 and 100 mg L−1 solutions of Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Li+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Sr2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ ions were prepared. The analysis involved preparing 2.0 mL mixtures of 1.0 mL of the CDs, 0.2 mL of the metal ion solution, and 0.8 mL of deionized water. Therefore, the mixture prepared had a final concentration of 5.0 mg L−1 of each metal ion under analysis and 1.10−3 mg L−1 of CDs. The respective mixture of each metal ion was manually stirred for about 20 s and left at rest for 5 min at room temperature. Then, the emission was measured at a wavelength of λ
ex = 380 nm.

The determination of CDs sensitivity continued for the ions that showed quenching of CDs fluorescence. The experiment consisted of the same procedure adopted previously, differing only in the concentration of metallic ions, which was varied between 0.0 and 50.0 mg L−1 (modifying the volume of the ion solution and deionized water used when preparing the 2.0 mL mixture with the volume of the CDs solution used remaining constant, 1.0 mL) that was analyzed with relation to fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated using Equations (1) and (2), respectively (Agência Nacional de Vigilância Sanitária (ANVISA), 2017b; Gong et al., 2015).

where σ is the standard deviation of blank intensity (n = 10), and m is the slope of the analytical curve.

To evaluate the performance of the CDs in photocatalysis, CDs_30, a sample that showed a higher emission intensity, was dispersed in commercial TiO2 (≥99%, nanoparticles, ReagentPlus/Sigma-Aldrich). A mixture of the two materials was prepared in the proportion of 95% (w/w) of TiO2 to CDs with deionized water, which was stirred at 800 rpm in a reactor (PARR, 4848) for 20 h. Subsequently, the resulting material was filtered and washed with 100 mL of deionized water and 50 mL of ethyl alcohol (Neon, 95% PA), and the resulting solid was dried in an oven for 8 h at 80 °C, named TiO2/CDs.

TiO2/CDs composite was characterized by Raman spectroscopy. In addition, diffuse reflectance spectrometry (DRS- UV–visible spectrophotometer, Shimadzu) was used to obtain the ultraviolet–visible absorption of the solid. The bandgap energies were determined using a Tauc plot, expressed in Equation (3).
(3)

)

= A

)
where α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, ѵ is the light frequency, n is the Tauc exponent (depends on the type of dominant electronic transition, where n = 2 for indirect), A is the proportionality constant, and E
g is the optical band gap (Haryński et al., 2022).

Photodegradation experiments of VBB dye were carried out in a homemade photocatalytic reactor consisting of four lamps (Philips Blacklight Blue-18W) as the UV light source (λ
max = 360 nm). Reactions were carried out without and with 30 min of light off to reach an equilibrium in adsorption/desorption. Then, the UV lamps were turned on, starting photocatalysis. For the reaction, 45 mg of the photocatalyst and 90 mL of 20 mg L -1 VBB solution were used, kept under stirring at 800 rpm. Aliquots were removed at predetermined times and centrifuged at 7200 rpm for 5 min to separate the supernatant, which was analyzed by UV–visible spectroscopy equipment (Jasco V-730).

The adsorption kinetics (reaction without the use of light) and blank test (photolysis) were evaluated under the same conditions. Furthermore, the reuse of the material was assessed through four cycles of adsorption and photocatalysis, with the photocatalytic reaction being carried out without a period in the dark. In each reuse cycle, the solid was washed once with deionized water and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. As the remaining mass solid decreased, the solution volume in each cycle was adjusted according to the mass photocatalyst/volume contaminant ratio of 0.5/1.

The CDs synthesis was conducted using microwave heating at different reaction times. The glycerol polymerization involves several steps, including dehydration, pyrolysis, and cross-linking (Huang et al., 2014a). This transformation was observed in the CG solution, which became a dark-colored and dense material after microwave treatment, as illustrated in Figure S1 (a) of the Supplementary Material.

Considering the photophysical results of CDs_21 and CDs_30 (presented at the end of this section), where both CDs exhibit similar absorbance and fluorescence spectra, but with CDs_30 showing a higher intensity, there is no need to present characterization results for both materials. Therefore, the following characterizations will focus on CDs_30.

Fig. 1(a and b) shows a TEM image in which the size and shape of CDs_30 are verified. The particles exhibited a well-dispersed spherical morphology with a few agglomerated areas. Fig. 1 (c) presents the size distribution histogram of the nanoparticles, derived from the HRTEM images provided in Fig. S2. The nanoparticles have an average diameter of 2.6 ± 0.3 nm, with sizes ranging from 1.8 to 3.4 nm. Results in the same size range were found in other studies, such as CDs synthesized with pure glycerol and citric acid in the presence of cow urine, which had an average size of 2–5 nm (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). Furthermore, proposed CDs_30 presented an average size lower than other CDs prepared from pure glycerol related in literature, 13.2 nm (Uriarte et al., 2019), 6.1 nm (Gao et al., 2018a), and 7.7 ± 1.5 nm (Noun et al., 2021). In SAED patterns, it was possible to trace six areas of the electron diffraction, being that two with d-spacing calculated of 3.3 and 2.1 Å corresponded at (002) and (100) planes of the graphitic structure identified in the XRD analysis (Calderon et al., 2016).

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR and 1H NMR spectra of CG and CDs_30. The broad peak at 3301 cm−1 in Fig. 2 (a) is associated with the stretching vibration of the O–H groups, which showed a considerable reduction in CDs compared to CG. The 1300-1000 cm−1 bands represent the C–O vibration due to the ethers. The band centered at 1563 cm−1 is related to the presence of C=C stretching, and the formation of an aromatic ring was also confirmed by the presence of a band between 3150 and 3000 cm−1 region of aromatic C-H stretching, with bending vibration present between 900 and 690 cm−1. The 2931 cm− 1 and 2848 cm−1 peaks correlate with the C-H sp3 stretching vibration and the band in the 1000–650 cm−1 with C-H bending vibration. The bending vibrations of CH2 and CH3 groups and the peaks at 1451 and 1403 cm−1, respectively, were also identified, referring to the polymer chain growth of glyceride groups (Pavia et al., 2016). CDs FTIR results showed a decrease in bands of O-H stretching vibration from glycerol by CDs. In addition, the C–H stretching (3000–2800 cm−1) and C–O stretching (1113 cm−1) in the CDs had a structure similar that of CDs_30 (Gao et al., 2018b). Furthermore, the stretching vibration of C=C is related to the CDs structure, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures (Gao et al., 2020).

The use of 1H NMR spectroscopy to identify the structure of CDs is essential because of their ability to provide detailed information about the hydrogen atoms in the sample. As small carbon-based nanoparticles, CDs often have functional groups on their surface that contribute to their unique properties. Thus, Fig. 2 (b) presents the high-resolution 1H NMR spectrum of CDs_30 exhibiting sp3 C−H proton peaks in the region of 1–2 ppm; ether protons are shown in the 3−4 ppm range due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups (alcohols, esters, or carboxylic acids). Aromatic sp2 and aldehyde protons occurred in the 7−9 ppm region, corroborating the formation of CDs (Thulasi et al., 2020). According to the NMR results, small molecules containing oxygenated groups, as different unreacted oligomers, were verified due to non-purification (3−4 ppm) of the material by dialysis, which was also verified by the non-purification of the material (Arroyave et al., 2021).

Synthesis of CDs using the bottom-up method requires precursors with specific polymerizable elements, such as dehydratable functional groups, crosslinking sites, or unsaturated additive bonds. Proper pyrolysis could facilitate the transformation of glycerol into CDs, as Arroyave et al. verified for their CDs produced based on glucose (Arroyave et al., 2021). Using microwaves and short reaction times results in minimal or incomplete polymerization, which can explain the large number of glyceride groups observed in 1H NMR. Based on these results, CDs seem to be polymers of carbon dots (PCDs), which consist of a low-carbonized core surrounded by polymers, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (c).

In the Raman scattering of CDs_30, Fig. 3 (a) shows that bands D and G are located in the regions with peaks at ∼1331 cm⁻1 and ∼1640 cm⁻1, respectively. The D band is related to the scattering of structural defects and the G band is associated with the vibration of the sp2–bonded C atoms of the graphitic structure (Cheng et al., 2013). Also, it was possible to identify the band in 1485 cm⁻1 peak that could correspond to the vibrational bending mode of CH2, reported in the Raman spectra of crude glycerol (Velázquez-Hernández et al., 2020), which is an additional indicative of incomplete polymerization and presence of glyceride groups described in 1H NMR analysis. For this reason, the formation of CDs was also confirmed using a more sensitive technique, such as EPR (Fig. 3 (b)), which can detect paramagnetic species in sp2 conjugated groups in the CDs.

Fig. 3 (b) shows a sharp resonance transition line (ΔBpp = 3 G or 0.3 mT) with a g-factor of approximately 2.01. This signal is characteristic of delocalized unpaired π-electrons in the highly conjugated polyaromatic structure of carbonaceous materials (Coleman et al., 2000; Insinna et al., 2023; Vorobiev, 2023; Vorobiev et al., 2020), strongly supporting the formation of CDs. The g-factor deviation to values greater than 2.00 is due to the interaction of these electrons with heteroatoms in the structure, such as oxygen (Krasnovyd et al., 2023; Retcofsky, 1982), which is reasonable considering that glycerol is used as a precursor in the synthesis of these CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XDR) profile of CDs_30 (Fig. 3 (c)) exhibited diffraction peaks in 2θ = 19.5° and 40.6°, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes of the graphitic structure, respectively. The weak and broad peaks were attributed to the amorphous carbon structure of the CDs (Du et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2014b).

Optical properties are fundamental characteristics of fluorescent nanomaterials, crucial in establishing their suitability for sensing applications (He et al., 2024). CDs fluorescence was examined using ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. Absorption, emission, and excitation analyses were performed in the electromagnetic spectral region between 200 and 800 nm. Fig. 4 (a) shows the absorbance spectra of the materials.

CDs_21 and CDs_30 materials present absorption peaks close to 224 nm, which can be associated with the π→π∗ transition of C=C, and 260 nm, which corresponds to the n→π∗ transition of C=O (Arroyave et al., 2021; He et al., 2024; Hu et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2014b; Thulasi et al., 2020). Although the spectra of the two materials were similar, CDs_30 exhibited a higher absorbance intensity, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The same pattern was observed in the fluorescence spectra (Figure (b)).

Fig. 4 (c) and (d) show the fluorescence emission spectra for CDs_21 and CDs_30, respectively. Both materials exhibit fluorescence emissions at different excitation wavelengths (λex = 240 nm–400 nm). It can also be observed that with increasing excitation length, the fluorescence peak shifts to longer wavelengths (red shift), which may indicate a nucleus of graphitic carbon, according to the literature (Tyagi et al., 2016) for the two CDs.

The maximum emission of CDs_30 was observed at λem = 366 nm when excited at λex = 240 nm (Fig. 5 (a) ). The broad emission band covers the UV and visible (length greater than 380 nm) spectral regions. Fig. 5 (b) illustrates the fluorescence of CDs under UV lamp irradiation.

The fluorescence quantum yield, determined by using quinine sulfate as a reference, according to reference, was 1.3 % at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm for CDs_30. Table 1 shows the QY of CDs_30 compared with other CDs prepared with glycerol carbon source. The QY of CD was found to be the lowest among all materials. These reasons can explain the result, such as the presence of impurities in the crude glycerol, which reduces the amount of glycerol effectively available for synthesizing CDs, which does not occur in the other CDs in Table 1. In addition, carrying out a purification step (dialysis), where such impurities can be minimized, may also contribute to a higher QY, as observed in the CDs proposed by Uriarte et al. (2019). Another point is the use of doping agents that catalyze the formation of CDs, as verified by Wang et al., where an increase in phosphate salts increases the photoluminescence intensity and QY of the CDs (Wang et al., 2011).

Sensitivity tests were conducted to verify the role of CDs_30 as a sensor and the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs_30 (Fig. 6 (a) ) upon reaction with different metal ions. When were compared the fluorescence of pure CDs (blank) with CDs + ions (referring to each ion tested), the lowest fluorescence signal, referring to the CDs + Fe3+ reaction, was observed that Fe3+ caused a stronger fluorescence quenching effect among the tested ions, as shown in Fig. 6 (b). According to the literature, this behavior is explained by the coordination between Fe3+ ions and phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of CDs (Gong et al., 2015). Fluorescence quenching occurs because of intense interaction between Fe3+ ions and the surface groups, leading to the transfer of photoelectrons from the CDs to Fe3+ ions (Gong et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2022; Varman et al., 2022). Furthermore, a quenching effect was observed in the presence of Cu2+ and Zn2+ for CDs_30. Moreover, an enhanced fluorescence intensity was observed in the presence of other metal ions, a mechanism that can also be used to sense metal ions (Tang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2017).

After verifying that the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs_30 was more prominent for Fe3+ and Cu2+ cations, the sensitivity of CDs_30 was studied by varying the concentration of these ions. Fig. 7 (a) shows CDs_30 fluorescence at different Fe3+ concentrations. The intensity of the CDs_30 emission peak exhibited an increase in extinction (decline in fluorescence intensity) as the Fe3+ concentration increased, with extinction becoming noticeable at concentrations starting from 1.0 mg L−1 of Fe3+. Fig. 7 (b) shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence peak shift (I/I
0 ) and Fe3+ concentrations. I and I
0 are the fluorescence intensities of CDs_30 with λ
ex = 380 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively. The I/I
0 for Fe3+ exhibited an excellent linear relationship ranging from 0.0 to 15.0 mg L−1, with R
2 = 0.996.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the sensitivity of CDs_30 fluorescence at different concentrations of Cu2+. The intensity of the emission peak indicated a decline in the fluorescence intensity as the Cu2+ concentration increased. However, this decline was less pronounced than that observed for Fe3+ (Fig. 7). In the literature, the quenching of CDs fluorescence by Cu2+ is explained by the complexation of Cu2+ ions with carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs (Murugan et al., 2019). The linear relationship between the fluorescence peak shift (I/I
0 ) and Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 8 (b)) had an excellent linear relationship ranging from 0.0 to 25.0 mg L−1, with R
2 = 0.993.

The quenching effect observed in Figs. 7 and 8 can be attributed to the interactions between the metal ions and the surface functional groups of the CDs, leading to an electron or energy transfer process. Cu2⁺ and Fe³⁺ are well-known to have strong affinities for oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, commonly found on the surface of CDs. When these metal ions interact with the CDs, they form coordination bonds, which facilitate an electron transfer from the excited state of the CDs to the metal ions. This electron transfer quenches the fluorescence by reducing the energy available for radiative decay. In addition, Fe³⁺ and Cu2⁺ ions can also act as paramagnetic species, leading to non-radiative decay processes (Bao et al., 2011; Guo et al., 2015; Tao et al., 2012).

When multiple metal ions are present, their interaction with the surface functional groups of the CDs can lead to competition for binding sites. This competition may enhance or reduce the quenching effect, depending on the nature and concentration of the ions involved. For example, as mentioned previously, metal ions such as Cu2⁺ and Fe³⁺ are known for their quenching solid ability due to efficient electron transfer and spin-orbit coupling mechanisms. If these ions are present in the mixture, they may dominate the quenching process, reducing the fluorescence intensity. Additionally, other ions might create a synergistic or antagonistic effect. Depending on their specific interactions with the CDs, specific ions could either increase or mitigate the quenching effect. These interactions can influence the electron transfer or energy transfer processes, leading to changes in fluorescence behavior (Ozyurt et al., 2023; Shabbir et al., 2023).

The LOD and LOQ of CDs_30 for Fe3+ and Cu2+ are listed in Table 2 . The LOD values indicate the smallest amount of ions that could be detected, showing that CDs_30 had higher sensitivity to Fe3+ than Cu2+ ions. This trend was also observed in the LOQ values, which represent the minimum amount of ions that can be quantified (Agência Nacional de Vigilância Sanitária (ANVISA), 2017a; Gong et al., 2015). For Brazil Resolution of CONAMA (Brazilian National Environmental Council), which provides for the conditions and standards for releasing effluents, the maximum permitted values for dissolved iron and copper are 15.0 mg L−1 and 1.0 mg L−1, respectively ((CONAMA), 2011). The iron and copper limits for US EPA (United States Environmental Protection Agency) are 0.3 and 1.0 mg L−1, respectively (US EPA, 2024). The values of 0.2 and 2.0 mg L−1 are determined in the European Union for iron and copper, respectively (UE, 2023). Considering these values, it can be inferred that the analytical curve obtained for Fe3+ presented sensitivity LOD and LOQ lower than the CONAMA maximum value allowed for Fe3+, indicating the potential role of CDs_30 in detecting Fe3+ in wastewater samples. It is still necessary to carry out more CDs_30 selectivity studies to verify the possible interference of these ions in wastewater or if it could be improved for other metallic ions.

Comparing CDs_30 sensing results with other CDs in the literature indicates that the proposed linear range is rarely reported, with linearities using lower concentrations between 0.0 and 6.4 mg. L−1 (or 0.0–100 μmmol) for the Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions (Atchudan et al., 2019; Murugan et al., 2019; Varman et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023) being more common. However, some CDs with a linear sensing range similar to CDs_30 were observed, as indicated in Table 2.

CDs have promising and versatile applications in environmental processes. Their unique properties and potential for modification make them an exciting area of photocatalytic research. CDs_30 was selected for dispersion in TiO2 to verify the influence of CDs_30 on the photocatalysis of this semiconductor. Fig. 9 (a) shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of the solid TiO2/CDs.

It was observed that TiO2/CDs maintained the absorption characteristics of pure TiO2, which occurred at wavelengths below 400 nm in the same position in the TiO2/CDs spectrum. The absorption edge of the second peak showed a small displacement for longer wavelengths in TiO2/CDs.

Concerning the calculated bandgap energy (Fig. 9(b) and (c)), TiO2/CDs had slightly lower band gap energy values than TiO2 (3.20 and 3.23 eV, respectively). This minor reduction could be attributed to the low CDs content, approximately 5%. Notably, there was no increase in the band gap energy, which would hinder the photocatalytic reaction.

Several factors can influence the band gap of CDs, such as their structure, nanoparticle size, and the presence of heteroatoms. In the literature, CDs synthesized differently, although with similar sizes, produced CDs with various degrees of crystallinity and elemental compositions, resulting in band gaps between 2.63 and 3.63 eV, calculated considering direct transition (Ibarbia et al., 2020).

Fig. 10 (a) shows the Raman spectra of pure TiO2 and the composite TiO2/CDs. Bands at 145 cm−1, 200 cm−1, 397 cm−1, 517 cm−1, and 641 cm−1 were identified in both the TiO2 and TiO2/CDs samples, which agrees with the vibrational mode Raman actives: Eg(1), Eg(2), B1g(1), A1g and Eg(3) of the TiO2 anatase phase, respectively (Balachandran and Eror, 1982; Ohsaka et al., 1978). The characteristic scattering of CDs was observed in the spectra of the TiO2/CDs between 1200 and 1800 cm−1 (Shen et al., 2018). Fig. 10 (b) illustrates the possible structure produced and the distribution of TiO2 in the CDs in the composite TiO2/CDs.

Fig. 11 shows the photolysis and photocatalysis reactions of VBB with CDs_30, TiO2/CDs, and TiO2. As shown in Fig. 11(a), the photolysis of VBB was negligible under the conditions analyzed. In contrast, in the presence of CDs, photocatalysis removed about 20% of the VBB after 60 min. The TiO2/CDs heterostructure demonstrated significantly higher photocatalytic efficiency than pure TiO2 within the first 30 min, with 78% and 72% removal rates, respectively. In addition, 98% degradation occurred after 150 min of the reaction.

When performing photocatalyst reuse cycles (Fig. 11 (b)), it was observed that from the first to the second photodegradation cycle, the VBB removal decreased from 93% to 67%, a reduction of 26%. In the adsorption reaction, the removal decreased from 68% to 42%. This difference highlights the power of renewal of the photocatalyst surface when degrading the dye, where the photocatalytic activity is more effective than the adsorptive activity.

Fig. 12 illustrates the mechanism of degradation of the VBB with the TiO2/CDs structure. Incorporating CDs into heterostructured materials has been reported to enhance the adsorption capacity of pollutants onto photocatalysts, thereby increasing the concentration of pollutants near the photocatalyst (Hu et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2018; Weilong Shi et al., 2018). Furthermore, CDs can be considered semiconductors; in this way, the excitation of CDs enables the generation of electrons/holes (Zhou et al., 2019). Fig. 12 shows this behavior and the role of CDs in the trapping and transferring of electrons (Long et al., 2021) generated by TiO2, helping the photogenerated charges reach the VBB dye.

First-order kinetics were calculated for the TiO2/CDs and TiO2 (Fig. S3). The values were 0.017 and 0.015 min−1, respectively. A comparison of the VBB degradation performance of TiO2/CDs and other photocatalysts is presented in Table 3 . TiO2/CDs showed a constant reaction rate of k higher than other photocatalysts using UV light. In addition, the degradation percentage was better when using a shorter time.

The stability of CD was tested under UV irradiation to verify the degradation materials after photocatalysis. Fig. 13 shows the photostability of CDs_30 measured using UV lamps (λ
max = 360 nm). The fluorescence intensity decreased by 30 % after irradiation for 60 min and remained stable for up to 180 min. Sun et al. verified in a similar experiment that quinine sulfate decreased the fluorescence intensity by approximately 40% after this same time (Sun et al., 2010). CDs_30 was more photostable than the quinine sulfate reference fluorescent molecule. Thus, these materials revealed a stabilist and can be dispersed into TiO2 for environmental applications.

This study demonstrates the successful synthesis of CDs from glycerol residues using microwave heating and explores their potential as sensors and photocatalysts. HRTEM analysis revealed well-dispersed spherical particles with an average size of 2.6 nm. FTIR and 1H NMR spectra indicated the presence of oxygen-containing groups and polyaromatic structures, suggesting partial polymerization of CG. Raman, EPR, and XRD analyses further confirmed the CDs structure, whereas UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy highlighted their optical properties. QY was calculated to be 1.3%. The synthesized CDs exhibit promising fluorescence properties with a significant quenching effect in the presence of Fe³⁺ and Cu2⁺ ions, suggesting their potential as sensitive sensors for these metal ions. The detection and quantification limits indicated a higher sensitivity to Fe³⁺ ions, aligned with the environmental standards for effluent monitoring. CDs enhanced the photocatalytic performance when combined with TiO2, likely due to increased pollutant adsorption near the photocatalyst. The TiO2/CDs composite showed improved photocatalytic efficiency compared with pure TiO2 and photostability, highlighting the beneficial role of CDs in environmental applications. This study confirmed the feasibility of using CG-derived CDs for sensing and photocatalysis, offering a sustainable environmental monitoring and remediation approach. Further research could explore optimizing synthesis conditions for enhancing CG residue polymerization and the applicability of CDs in various environmental contexts.
